lens.included_activities: Independent US firms providing repeat or recurring land, boundary, construction, topographic, aerial, GIS, photogrammetric, or mapping services to external customers in the lower-middle-market band.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive owner, contractor, utility, or government units; non-control financing vehicles; geophysical surveying; software-only mapping vendors; and firms outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project, task-order, hourly, unit, and occasionally retainer revenue from developers, contractors, utilities, governments, and property owners; repeat work is retained only where a firm continues to be outsourced.

a.rationale: S1 shows the broader 541300 industry has meaningful surveyor, cartographer, and surveying-technician employment, while S2 describes record search, measurement, verification, drafting, reporting, GIS, CAD, and client presentation. AI can reduce office interpretation, coding, drafting, QA triage, and administrative work, but field capture, boundary judgment, and legal review remain substantial. The range is a wage-weighted judgment, not a measured automation share.
a.change_evidence: A 541370 six-digit OEWS occupation-and-wage extract plus time-and-motion studies from land-survey firms separating field capture, office processing, licensed review, and administration.
caveats: The available industry occupation mix is NAICS 541300, not 541370.
No source measures current task-level AI substitution for the frozen lens.
The injected compensation-to-receipts ratio may be distorted because owner labor and subcontractors are not consistently represented in payroll.

rho.rationale: S2 documents work that can be digitized, but also field measurement, accuracy verification, legal records, and client-facing responsibility. S3 says licensing is required in all states and the District of Columbia to provide public surveying services and certify legal documents. Those controls make workflow implementation materially slower and narrower than technical capability alone.
rho.change_evidence: A representative 541370 software-use survey, implementation case studies with before-and-after labor hours, and state-board guidance on AI-assisted deliverables and sign-off.
caveats: State licensing and client acceptance differ materially.
This excludes demand, pricing, and valuation effects by construction.

e.rationale: The NAICS title and S2's descriptions point to externally supplied surveying and mapping work, but the injected LMM count can include firms with nonrepeat projects, mixed engineering businesses, captive-like arrangements, and service lines outside the frozen lens. The base allows for those exclusions while recognizing that the classification is primarily a service industry.
e.change_evidence: Business-register microdata or a D&B/Grata/SourceScrub list reviewed for receipts, ownership, customer type, and service mix; supplemented by firm interviews.
caveats: No public source measures lens eligibility among the injected LMM count.
Six-digit classification may not isolate mixed engineering-and-survey practices cleanly.

s5.rationale: S2 reports 3,900 average annual surveyor openings and attributes many to workers leaving the labor force, including retirement; S4 gives examples of recent acquisitions of surveying practices. These facts support a nonzero succession and transaction pipeline, but neither measures qualifying control transfers of eligible LMM firms, so the five-year probability is a bounded judgment.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year matched transaction census from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, and state licensure/firm-registration records, with deal type and revenue size verified.
caveats: Occupation exits are not business-owner retirements.
Press releases identify completed transactions but do not provide a denominator or represent the LMM population.
Deaths, closures, internal reorganizations, and minority investments are excluded from the construct but not measured here.

q.rationale: The lens uses project, hourly, task-order, and unit billing. Office efficiency can be retained during a fixed-price engagement, but competitive bids, hourly billing, public procurement, renewals, and customer expectations of lower turnaround costs should share a meaningful portion of benefits. The base assumes a mixed book rather than full pass-through or full retention.
q.change_evidence: An anonymized sample of 541370 proposals, MSAs, task orders, utilization reports, and win-loss data showing billing basis, price resets, and margins before and after automation.
caveats: No public industry source reports contract-form shares or AI-related repricing.
Retention will vary sharply between regulated boundary work, construction support, government work, and commodity mapping.

d5.bridge: S2 measures US employment of the surveyor occupation across employers, not constant-price demand for the 541370 lens. Its 4 percent 2024-34 projection is converted to an approximate 2 percent five-year change and widened for cyclicality and for occupation demand differing from service quantity. Because productivity may allow employment to understate output, the proxy can understate demand; construction exposure can also make it overstate a recession-period service basket.
d5.rationale: S2 projects surveyor employment from 56,100 in 2024 to 58,600 in 2034 and identifies continuing boundary certification, construction-site review, road repair, and mining needs, while noting drones and other technology can limit employment growth. That supports roughly stable to modestly growing service demand over five years, with a broad interval for construction and real-estate cyclicality.
d5.change_evidence: BEA/Census service-output or receipts series at 541370, bid-volume data from public agencies, and a customer-segment revenue panel deflated for service price and quality changes.
caveats: The source is an occupation projection, not six-digit industry output or quantity demand.
The source's horizon is ten years and includes employment in other industries.

o.rationale: S2 says surveyors establish boundaries for legal documents, verify measurements, and work in the field; S3 says public surveying services and certification of legal documents require licensure in every state and the District of Columbia. Automation can shrink labor per job and enable self-service for lower-risk mapping, but a large share of the service basket should still need an accountable operator for legal, accuracy, and site-specific work.
o.change_evidence: State-by-state scope-of-practice review, customer interviews on willingness to accept unlicensed digital deliverables, and project-level data separating legal surveys from noncertified mapping.
caveats: Licensure protects only work within regulated scope and does not guarantee the same firm type supplies it.
Noncertified aerial, GIS, and visualization work may face faster software substitution than boundary surveys.

narrative.executive_view: Surveying and mapping combines repeat outsourced professional services with significant office-processing work, but its core legal and field-accountability functions constrain full automation. The evidence supports a measured opportunity rather than a purely software-substitution thesis.
narrative.ai_impact: AI and adjacent automation can assist record extraction, field-data coding, point-cloud and imagery processing, drafting, map revision, QA triage, reporting, and scheduling. Precise field measurement, resolving conflicts among records and site conditions, and licensed certification remain key human tasks.
narrative.value_capture: A mixed project, task-order, hourly, and unit-priced book permits retention of some efficiency on fixed-price work, while competitive bids, public procurement, and renewals pressure the operator to share savings with customers. Contract review is essential before treating technical savings as margin.
narrative.firm_availability: Surveyor openings attributable in part to retirement and recent practice acquisitions indicate succession and transaction activity, but neither is a denominator for LMM control transfers. The transfer estimate therefore remains deliberately broad.
narrative.demand_durability: Boundary certification, construction-site review, infrastructure maintenance, and mapping needs create durable operator demand. BLS projects 4 percent surveyor employment growth from 2024 to 2034, while explicitly noting that drones and related technology may limit employment growth.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The best available occupation mix is a four-digit architecture-and-engineering proxy rather than 541370. Construction and property-market cyclicality, state licensing differences, mixed-firm classification, subcontractor use, and scarce contract-form and transaction data are material uncertainties.
narrative.principal_driver: Licensed, accountable boundary and site work remains necessary even as office processing becomes more automated.
narrative.principal_weakness: There is no direct public measurement of six-digit task exposure, transferable-firm incidence, contract pass-through, or constant-quality service demand.

sources.title: BLS May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates, NAICS 541300
sources.supports: Broader architectural, engineering, and related-services occupation mix: surveyors 38,520, cartographers and photogrammetrists 2,300, and surveying and mapping technicians 34,920.
